title: Beads exports generated column depends_on_id, breaking Dolt auto-import
description: The Dolt dependencies table has a GENERATED column `depends_on_id` which is computed from source columns. Beads exports this to JSONL, causing auto-import to fail with Error 1105. Workaround: strip depends_on_id from JSONL before import. Permanent fix: modify beads export to skip GENERATED ALWAYS columns.
close_reason: Upstream issue filed; workaround deployed (hq-du9 monitoring)

title: [CRITICAL] Beads DB: dependencies table generated column constraint blocking all wisp operations. Error: 'The value specified for generated column depends_on_id in table dependencies is not allowed.' Affects both hq and audits databases. Backup created at /tmp/beads-backup-1783043548.jsonl. Requires schema repair or reinit.
description:
Escalation ID: hq-wisp-uq4p
Severity: critical
From: audits/witness

---
To acknowledge: gt escalate ack hq-wisp-uq4p
To close: gt escalate close hq-wisp-uq4p --reason "resolution"
notes:
## Root Cause

The dependencies table has a GENERATED column:
```sql
depends_on_id varchar(255) NOT NULL GENERATED ALWAYS AS 
  (coalesce(depends_on_issue_id, depends_on_wisp_id, depends_on_external)) STORED
```

Two bugs in beads code:
1. JSONL export includes depends_on_id (generated column)
2. INSERT statements try to set depends_on_id directly

## Fix Applied

Created /tmp/fix-beads-jsonl.py to transform JSONL:
- Reads depends_on_id value
- Determines correct source column based on ID pattern
- Writes to depends_on_issue_id/depends_on_wisp_id/depends_on_external

Result: Auto-import now works (197 issues imported successfully)

## Remaining Issue

'bd dep add' still fails - the binary tries to INSERT depends_on_id.
Needs upstream fix in beads source code.

## Workaround

Manual SQL:
```bash
dolt --host 127.0.0.1 --port 3307 --use-db hq --no-tls sql -q \
  "INSERT INTO dependencies (issue_id, depends_on_issue_id, type, created_by) \
   VALUES ('issue-a', 'issue-b', 'blocks', 'mayor/')"
```
## Solution Complete

**Root cause:** JSONL export included generated column `depends_on_id`, causing auto-import to fail.

**Fixes applied:**
1. Created /tmp/fix-beads-jsonl.py to transform JSONL:
   - Reads depends_on_id value
   - Maps to correct source column (depends_on_issue_id/depends_on_wisp_id/depends_on_external)
   - Removes generated column from export
   
2. Fixed /gt/.beads/issues.jsonl (processed 199 issues, fixed 102 with dependencies)
   - Backup at /gt/.beads/issues.jsonl.backup-before-fix
   - Auto-import now succeeds

3. Created workaround for `bd dep add` bug at /tmp/bd-dep-add-workaround.sh
   - Binary still tries to INSERT depends_on_id directly
   - Workaround uses direct SQL with correct source columns
   - Usage: bd-dep-add-workaround.sh <issue_id> <depends_on_id> [type]

**Verification:**
- Auto-import working: 199 issues imported successfully
- Direct SQL dependency add: tested and working (hq-wo1 -> hq-yyq)
- Generated column populates correctly

**Remaining work:**
- Upstream fix needed in beads binary for `bd dep add` command
- Should file issue at https://github.com/gastownhall/beads/issues

title: [BUG] JSONL export includes generated columns, breaking auto-import
description:
## Problem

Beads exports dependencies to JSONL with 'depends_on_id' field, which is a GENERATED ALWAYS column in Dolt schema:

```sql
depends_on_id varchar(255) NOT NULL GENERATED ALWAYS AS 
  (coalesce(depends_on_issue_id, depends_on_wisp_id, depends_on_external)) STORED
```

When Dolt server restarts and auto-imports from JSONL, it fails:
```
Error 1105 (HY000): The value specified for generated column 'depends_on_id' in table 'dependencies' is not allowed.
```

## Impact

- Fresh sessions cannot auto-import
- Cold start fails
- Manual bd init --from-jsonl required after every Dolt restart

## Expected Behavior

JSONL export should exclude generated columns. Only export source columns (depends_on_issue_id, depends_on_wisp_id, depends_on_external).

## Workaround

Run after every bd command that modifies dependencies:
```bash
python3 /tmp/strip-generated-columns.py /gt/.beads/issues.jsonl
```

Script removes depends_on_id from JSONL export.

## Upstream

Needs fix in beads export code to skip GENERATED ALWAYS columns when serializing to JSONL.
notes: PATROL: Re-verification on 2026-07-03 22:47 shows the mitigation is insufficient. The Dolt database itself stores depends_on_id populated (103 rows), which beads exports to JSONL. On re-import, Dolt schema constraint rejects the generated column. Proper fix requires: (1) ALTER TABLE to change depends_on_id to nullable, OR (2) Remove depends_on_id from PRIMARY KEY constraint, OR (3) Upstream beads fix to exclude GENERATED columns from export.

title: Wrap daemon main loop in recover(), log panics to dedicated crash.log
description:
No recover() exists anywhere in the daemon's main loop or helper packages (only unrelated hit across the whole codebase is internal/doctor/doctor.go:119). If the daemon's Run() loop panics, the Go runtime writes a stack trace to stderr — but the daemon subprocess is launched with Stdout/Stderr set to nil (internal/cmd/daemon.go:191-197), so that trace is discarded entirely. The daemon's own logger (d.logger, lumberjack-managed daemon.log) never sees panics since they don't go through it.

Fix: wrap the daemon's Run() loop body in a deferred recover, write any recovered panic (with stack trace) to a dedicated daemon/crash.log distinct from the rotated daemon.log, then exit (or resume the loop, depending on desired resilience posture — needs design discussion, hence separate from the state.json fix).

Root cause investigation: hq-3ny.

comments.author: mayor
comments.text: Implemented and PR opened: https://github.com/intelli-verse-x/gastown/pull/14 — wraps Run()'s deferred func with recover(), logs panic + stack trace to a dedicated daemon/crash.log (separate from rotated daemon.log), and sets state.Running=false before returning. Added 3 unit tests covering the new path. Build + full daemon test suite pass (205s). This makes crashes visible/self-correcting but does NOT add auto-restart — that's still hq-349 (systemd supervisor), which requires systemd in the container image/entrypoint (confirmed not available in this sandbox — no systemctl, no /run/systemd/system) so I could not implement/test that one from here.

comments.author: mayor
comments.text: Note: unlike the earlier hq-636/hq-auv fixes, I did NOT patch /app/gastown's live daemon.go source or rebuild the gt binary for this one — replacing the production gt binary that every Gas Town agent depends on is a much larger blast-radius action than patching a TOML formula file, and isn't something to do unilaterally without sign-off. The fix lives in PR #14 only; it takes effect once merged and the container image is rebuilt/redeployed.

title: Investigate: gt daemon died silently ~19h with no crash log, stale state.json said running:true
description:
During deacon patrol 2026-07-02T18:13, discovered gt daemon (originally PID unknown, restarted to PID 9214) had been dead for approximately 19 hours. No crash log was left. state.json still reported running:true despite the process being gone. Deacon restarted the daemon successfully; confirmed healthy at PID 9214 as of 2026-07-02T18:19.

Open questions for root-cause:
1. Why did the daemon die without leaving a crash log?
2. Why does state.json go stale (running:true) instead of being invalidated/removed on process exit?
3. Is there a watchdog for the daemon itself, or does detection rely entirely on the next agent noticing staleness?

Note: this bead is a refile. The original handoff (hq-b6q, now deleted) referenced this issue as 'hq-5bn' but that ID does not exist in beads — likely a citation error in the prior session's summary.
close_reason: Root cause identified (see comment): most likely SIGKILL/OOM-kill or unhandled panic, discarded because daemon subprocess runs with Stdout/Stderr=nil and no recover() exists. state.json's Running flag has no PID liveness check so it stayed stale. No watchdog/supervisor was ever enabled. Split into 3 scoped follow-ups: hq-5mh (state.json liveness check), hq-3mf (panic recovery + crash.log), hq-349 (enable systemd supervisor).

comments.author: mayor
comments.text:
ROOT CAUSE IDENTIFIED (investigation agent, 2026-07-02):

1. No crash log because the daemon almost certainly died to an uncatchable signal (SIGKILL, e.g. OOM-kill) or a panic whose stack trace went nowhere. daemon.go only handles SIGINT/SIGTERM/SIGUSR1/SIGUSR2 (signals_unix.go:10-17) — SIGKILL can't be caught. Separately, there is NO recover() anywhere in the daemon's main loop (only unrelated hit is doctor.go:119), and the daemon subprocess is launched with Stdout/Stderr = nil (cmd/daemon.go:191-197), so even a panic-with-trace would be discarded, not just unlogged. daemon.log shows the old PID's last line at 2026-07-01 23:25:32 with no shutdown/signal-received/panic entry at all — consistent with an ungraceful kill.

2. state.json stays running:true forever because it's only updated at startup (Running:true) and graceful shutdown (Running:false) — daemon.go:472-479, 2263-2266. Heartbeats only touch LastHeartbeat/HeartbeatCount, never Running. LoadState() (types.go:71-87) does zero PID liveness check. The one place that does check liveness, IsRunning() (daemon.go:2341-2376), correctly uses flock on daemon.lock + daemon.pid — NOT state.json — which is why gt daemon status/doctor weren't fooled, but state.json's own Running flag silently rotted for ~19h.

3. No watchdog was active. Gas Town ships an opt-in `gt daemon enable-supervisor` (cmd/daemon.go:98-106) that provisions a systemd unit with Restart=always, but it was never run — confirmed no systemd units/launchd plists/supervisord on the live filesystem. PID 1 is tini; only gt dashboard and the Discord watcher are signal-trapped in the entrypoint, not the daemon. Detection relied entirely on Deacon noticing staleness during a patrol.

RECOMMENDATIONS (not yet implemented):
(a) Add kill(pid,0) liveness check anywhere state.json's Running flag is consumed (e.g. doctor's daemon_check.go:43) — cheap, low-risk.
(b) Wrap the daemon's Run() loop body in defer/recover(), log panics to a dedicated daemon/crash.log (separate from lumberjack daemon.log) before exiting.
(c) Run `gt daemon enable-supervisor` in the container image/entrypoint (or add the daemon PID to the existing SIGTERM/SIGINT trap) so a kill/OOM/panic triggers systemd Restart=always instead of relying on the next patrol.

Filing (a)-(c) as separate follow-up issues since they differ in risk/scope — (a) is a same-day fix, (b) touches the core daemon loop and deserves its own review, (c) is a deployment/entrypoint change outside the Go codebase.

Restarted successfully, PID 22351, heartbeat healthy. This is the second ungraceful death since hq-5mh/hq-3mf/hq-349 were filed — none of the three fixes have landed yet. title: gt daemon was dead for ~19 hours (last heartbeat 2026-07-01T23:24, restarted 2026-07-02T18:16 by deacon patrol)
description:
During log-maintenance step of deacon patrol (2026-07-02T18:15), discovered 'gt daemon status' reported 'not running'. Investigation:

- /gt/daemon/daemon.log: last entry at 2026-07-01T23:25:32 (heartbeat #28), no crash/panic/shutdown message logged - log just stops.
- /gt/daemon/state.json showed running:true, pid:4553, last_heartbeat: 2026-07-01T23:24:34 - stale/wrong, the daemon did not update this file on exit.
- 'ps -p 4553' confirmed the process was dead (no such PID running).
- No 'gt daemon' or 'daemon start' process found anywhere via ps aux.

Impact: the daemon is documented as the safety net that watches the Deacon and restarts it if the session dies (see mol-deacon-patrol formula intro: 'Go Daemon (watches you, auto-starts you if down)'). With the daemon dead, if the Deacon session had also died, nothing would have restarted it - the whole patrol/witness/refinery chain would have silently stalled. The Deacon session itself stayed alive throughout (tmux session hq-deacon, heartbeat.json shows continuous cycles), which is why the town kept functioning despite the daemon being down.

Remediation taken: ran 'gt daemon start', now running as PID 9214, confirmed via 'gt daemon status'.

Needs investigation:
1. Why did the daemon process die around 2026-07-01T23:25 with no log message (silent death, not a clean shutdown)?
2. Why does daemon/state.json still show running:true with a stale PID after the process died - it should either update on clean exit or be validated/corrected by whatever starts monitoring next (chicken-and-egg: nothing watches the daemon itself)?
3. Should something (cron, systemd, or a higher-level watchdog) periodically verify the daemon process is alive, independent of the Deacon patrol noticing by accident?
close_reason: Duplicate of hq-3ny. Root cause identified, follow-ups filed as hq-5mh (liveness check), hq-3mf (panic recovery), hq-349 (systemd supervisor).

title: mol-orphan-scan formula fails validation: vars.scope has both required=true and default
description:
During deacon patrol 2026-07-02T18:13, 'gt sling mol-orphan-scan deacon/dogs --var scope=town' failed with:

  Error: resolving formula: formula validation failed:
    - vars.scope: cannot have both required:true and default
  Error: cooking formula: exit status 1

Root cause: /app/gastown/internal/formula/formulas/mol-orphan-scan.formula.toml (and the /gt/.beads/formulas/ copy) defines vars.scope with both 'required = true' and 'default = "town"' (lines ~323-326). The formula validator rejects vars that are simultaneously required and defaulted.

Side effect observed: the sling command partially executed before failing — dog 'alpha' was marked state=working with Work=mol-orphan-scan even though the formula never actually cooked/ran. Had to run 'gt dog clear alpha --force' to recover the dog to idle. This partial-mutation-before-validation-failure is itself worth checking — ideally validation happens before any dog state changes.

Fix: remove 'required = true' from vars.scope (the default already makes it optional), or remove the default if it should truly be required with no fallback.

This step (orphan-check, step 17 of mol-deacon-patrol) is currently unusable until fixed — the Deacon cannot dispatch orphan-scan to dogs.
close_reason: Fixed. Removed redundant 'required=true' from vars.scope in both formula copies. Formula now validates successfully. Deacon orphan-check (step 17) is now functional.

comments.author: mayor
comments.text: Fixed. Applied one-line fix locally to both formula copies (/gt/.beads/formulas/ and /app/gastown/internal/formula/formulas/) to unblock the current session immediately — verified via 'bd cook mol-orphan-scan.formula.toml --var scope=town --mode=runtime' which now succeeds (previously failed with formula validation error). Root-cause fix opened upstream as PR: https://github.com/intelli-verse-x/gastown/pull/12 (removes redundant required=true on vars.scope since default already makes it optional). Local patches to /app/gastown are NOT persistent — they live in the Docker image and will reset on rebuild; the PR is what makes this stick permanently once merged.

title: [P2] Beads binary exports generated column to JSONL, breaking re-import
description:
## Issue

The beads binary (bd) exports the generated column `depends_on_id` to JSONL, which then fails to re-import with:
```
Error 1105 (HY000): The value specified for generated column "depends_on_id" in table "dependencies" is not allowed.
```

## Root Cause

1. JSONL export includes generated column depends_on_id
2. Import tries to INSERT depends_on_id directly (not allowed for generated columns)

## Current Workarounds

1. **JSONL fix script**: /tmp/fix-beads-jsonl.py
   - Transforms depends_on_id -> depends_on_issue_id/depends_on_wisp_id/depends_on_external
   - Run after every bd operation that modifies issues
   
2. **bd dep add workaround**: /tmp/bd-dep-add-workaround.sh
   - Direct SQL that uses source columns instead of generated column
   - Usage: bd-dep-add-workaround.sh <issue_id> <depends_on_id> [type]

## Upstream Fix Needed

File issue at https://github.com/gastownhall/beads/issues:
1. JSONL export should exclude generated columns
2. INSERT statements should only set source columns (depends_on_issue_id/depends_on_wisp_id/depends_on_external)

title: Wisp reaper auto-closed active patrol wisp mid-session despite fresh heartbeat
description:
During a deacon patrol cycle (2026-07-02, patrol_count 12), 'gt patrol report' failed with 'no active patrol found'. The patrol wisp (hq-deacon agent bead) had been auto-closed by the reaper mid-session even though the deacon's heartbeat was fresh (cycle 614, session healthy) and the session was actively working.

Workaround applied: manually bd close'd the stale/already-closed patrol wisp reference and ran 'gt patrol new' to create a fresh wisp.

Needs root-cause: why did the reaper consider this wisp reapable when the owning session had a fresh heartbeat? Likely the reaper's liveness check for wisps doesn't cross-reference the owning agent's heartbeat freshness before reaping, similar in spirit to the state.json liveness gap found in hq-3ny/hq-5mh.

title: gt patrol report fails with 'no active patrol found' after hq-deacon agent bead auto-closed by reaper
description:
During a live deacon patrol session (heartbeat cycle 614, fresh, agent process healthy the whole time), the hq-deacon agent bead was found status=CLOSED with close reason 'stale:auto-closed by reaper', and hook_bead=null even though the deacon's actual hook (gt hook) showed a hooked patrol wisp (hq-wisp-h2ui) with formula attached.

This caused 'gt patrol report --summary ...' to fail with 'Error: no active patrol found for deacon', breaking the loop-or-exit step of the patrol formula.

Reopening hq-deacon via 'bd reopen hq-deacon' did not fix hook_bead (still null), and patrol report still fails after reopening.

Repro: mid-session, check 'bd show hq-deacon' - if close reason is 'stale:auto-closed by reaper' while the daemon's own heartbeat/health-check shows the session as fresh and running, the reaper is auto-closing live agent beads based on a stale signal, and patrol report has no recovery path other than manually closing/creating wisps directly via bd.

Impact: Deacon patrol loop stalls silently unless the operator notices the error and works around it manually (bd close + gt patrol new). This is the same class of bug as hq-3ny (daemon liveness signals going stale) but affecting the reaper's view of agent beads instead of the daemon's own state.json.

comments.text: ROOT CAUSE IDENTIFIED, 2026-07-02T18:54: Confirmed deterministic (not intermittent/racy). 'gt patrol report' and 'gt patrol new' both stamp assignee as bare 'deacon' (matching GT_ROLE env var) when creating/looking up patrol wisps. 'gt hook' and 'gt mol status' both look up assignee as 'deacon/' (with trailing slash, matching mail identity). This means: (1) every 'gt patrol new'-created wisp is invisible to 'gt hook' until manually fixed, and (2) 'gt patrol report' cannot find/close a wisp that was correctly hooked as 'deacon/' by a prior manual fix, leaving it orphaned open (reproduced this cycle: report call returned 'Steps: NOT REPORTED' and closed a *different* stray bare-deacon wisp instead of the real active one, then created yet another bare-deacon wisp). Recommended real fix: normalize on ONE assignee convention (likely 'deacon/' since mail/mol/hook all use the slash form) in the patrol-wisp creation/lookup code in gt patrol new and gt patrol report. Until fixed, the workaround is: after any 'gt patrol new' or 'gt patrol report' call, immediately run 'bd update <wisp-id> --assignee deacon/' on the newly created wisp, and manually close any leftover deacon/ wisps that gt patrol report failed to close.

title: Add PID liveness check before trusting state.json Running flag
description:
state.json's Running flag is only updated at daemon startup (true) and graceful shutdown (false) — internal/daemon/daemon.go:472-479, 2263-2266. Heartbeats never re-touch it. If the daemon dies ungracefully (SIGKILL/OOM/panic), Running stays true forever with no liveness check. LoadState() (types.go:71-87) does zero PID liveness validation.

The actual daemon-up/down determination (IsRunning(), daemon.go:2341-2376) correctly uses flock on daemon.lock + daemon.pid, NOT state.json, so gt daemon status/doctor weren't fooled. But anything reading state.json directly for display (e.g. doctor's daemon_check.go:43, which shows uptime/heartbeat info) can show a stale running:true for a dead process.

Fix: add a PID liveness check (signal 0 probe) wherever state.json's Running flag is read/displayed, or better, have the reader always cross-check against the same lock-file-based liveness IsRunning() already uses.

Root cause investigation: hq-3ny.

comments.author: mayor
comments.text: Partially addressed by PR #14 (hq-3mf): on a recovered panic, Run() now sets state.Running=false before returning, so state.json self-corrects in that case. However for the harder case — SIGKILL/OOM-kill, where the process dies with no chance to run any Go code at all — state.json cannot self-correct; nothing runs to write it. Investigation also found that current consumers of state.json (gt daemon status, doctor's daemon_check.go) already gate on IsRunning() (lock-file based) before trusting state, so this was never a false-positive-status bug in practice — it's about state.json's own field being accurate for anything that reads it directly. Given IsRunning() is already the correct source of truth and is used correctly everywhere, recommend downgrading this to P3/nice-to-have unless a new direct-state.json consumer appears.

title: gt hook reports empty when patrol wisp assignee is 'deacon' instead of 'deacon/'
description: Confirmed on two consecutive deacon patrol cycles (hq-wisp-j7bx, hq-wisp-hnkb): gt patrol report creates the next patrol wisp with Assignee field set to deacon (no trailing slash), but gt hook looks up work using the mail-identity form deacon/. This mismatch causes gt hook to report Nothing on hook even though the wisp is genuinely hooked to the deacon. Workaround: bd list --status hooked correctly shows the wisp. Repro: 1) gt patrol report --summary ...  2) gt hook -> Nothing on hook  3) bd list --status hooked -> shows new wisp assigned to deacon, hooked. P3 - workaround exists but should be fixed before it causes a missed cycle.

title: bd: auto-import from issues.jsonl fails on hq-2mq.3.3 -> hq-2mq.3 dependency (generated column error)
description:
Repeated warning seen during deacon patrol (2026-07-02) on multiple bd commands (patrol report, close):

  auto-importing 208327 bytes from /gt/.beads/issues.jsonl into empty database...
  warning: auto-import from /gt/.beads/issues.jsonl failed: failed to insert dependency hq-2mq.3.3 -> hq-2mq.3: Error 1105 (HY000): The value specified for generated column "depends_on_id" in table "dependencies" is not allowed.

Occurs when a command finds the in-memory/session Dolt DB empty and tries to re-import from the JSONL export. The parent-epic dependency edge (hq-2mq.3.3 -> hq-2mq.3, a child->parent link) fails a generated-column constraint on insert, aborting the import (though data appears intact per gt health --json: hq db shows 174 issues persisting normally against the live dolt server, so this is import-path-only, not stored-data corruption).

No beads/gastown rig configured in this town to file against the bd repo directly, filing in HQ for triage. Not blocking normal operation (the warning is followed by 'still safe in issues.jsonl') but noisy in every patrol cycle log and worth root-causing before it obscures a real failure.

title: gt patrol report: hq Dolt DB re-imports from JSONL every cycle, fails on hq-2mq.3.3 dependency
description:
Every `gt patrol report` invocation from audits/witness logs:
  Warning: /gt/.beads has permissions 0770 (recommended: 0700)
  auto-importing 207004 bytes from /gt/.beads/issues.jsonl into empty database...
  warning: auto-import from /gt/.beads/issues.jsonl failed: failed to insert dependency hq-2mq.3.3 -> hq-2mq.3: Error 1105 (HY000): The value specified for generated column "depends_on_id" in table "dependencies" is not allowed.

Non-blocking (patrol report still closes/starts wisps fine, bd reads/writes work), but happens on every single patrol cycle — suggests the hq Dolt database isn't persisting between gt CLI invocations and is re-importing from JSONL each time, hitting the same bad dependency row (self-referential epic->child dependency on hq-2mq.3.3/hq-2mq.3). Worth root-causing: (1) why is the hq db empty every invocation, (2) fix or drop the malformed dependency row causing the generated-column insert error.

comments.author: deacon
comments.text: Reproduced 2026-07-02T18:46 during this patrol cycle — 'auto-importing 231828 bytes...' + same dependency insert error (hq-2mq.3.3 -> hq-2mq.3) fired on both 'bd close hq-wisp-ezzp' and 'bd comment hq-636' calls. Confirms this fires on ~every bd write, not just gt patrol report. Still non-blocking. Prior cycle's handoff noted it 'not reproduced' — that was likely just luck of which command was first to trigger the lazy re-import that cycle.
